instruction.global: Melt the pat of butter on the hot pan surface until it is surrounded by a large area of bubbling liquid.
steps: Generate a ring of white foam and translucent liquid butter around the base of the solid pat.
Expand the diameter of the bubbling liquid ring and slightly reduce the volume of the central solid butter.
Increase the density and size of the bubbles in the liquid butter while the central solid piece continues to shrink.

explain: Thermal energy from the pan breaks the solid structure of the butter at the contact point, creating a liquid phase with trapped air bubbles.
As the temperature rises, more butterfat melts and water content begins to sizzle, expanding the pool of liquid.
The phase transition continues, with the solid core diminishing as it turns into a bubbling, foaming liquid.

instruction.global: Show the candle burning down significantly, reducing its length and causing wax to drip down the side.
steps: Slightly shorten the candle as it burns and curve the wick further.
Continue the burning process, further reducing the candle's length and smoothing the wax rim around the flame.
Significantly shorten the candle and add prominent wax drips running down the side.

explain: Combustion of the wick and wax vapor causes the candle body to slowly decrease in height.
Heat from the flame melts the surrounding paraffin, creating a liquid pool and further lowering the solid candle level.
As the candle burns down, the accumulated molten wax overflows the rim and solidifies into drips along the side due to gravity.
invariants: The diagonal orientation of the candle
White color of the candle wax

instruction.global: Use a hot soldering iron to melt the tip of a solder wire, leaving a molten bead on the iron as it is pulled away.
steps: Bring a hot soldering iron tip into contact with the end of the solder wire, initiating the release of smoke.
Maintain contact until the tip of the wire melts into a liquid bead that adheres to the soldering iron.
Pull the soldering iron away from the wire, keeping the molten solder bead on the iron's tip while smoke continues to rise.

explain: Thermal energy transfers from the iron to the solder, causing the internal flux to vaporize and create smoke.
The solder reaches its melting point and undergoes a phase change from solid to liquid, wetting the metal tip of the iron.
Surface tension holds the liquid solder to the iron tip as it moves, while the remaining wire tip begins to cool.
invariants: Camera perspective and fixed focal plane.
The position of the black component at the base of the wire.

instruction.global: Pour hot water over the dry coffee grounds in the filter to initiate the bloom process.
steps: Begin pouring a thin stream of water onto the center of the dry coffee grounds.
Continue pouring water in a circular motion to saturate the grounds, causing them to swell and rise.
Stop the water flow and allow the coffee to bloom, with bubbles forming on the surface of the wet slurry.

explain: Water makes initial contact with the dry grounds, starting the wetting process and darkening the coffee.
The grounds absorb water and release trapped carbon dioxide, causing the coffee bed to expand and lift.
Surface tension and gas release create bubbles on the surface as the water begins to permeate the filter.
invariants: Position and metallic texture of the dripper
Shape and position of the paper filter

instruction.global: Melt the scoops of chocolate and vanilla ice cream until they lose their structural integrity and form a large, viscous puddle on the dark surface.
steps: Begin melting the ice cream scoops, causing liquid to pool around the base and softening the sharp edges of the chocolate portion.
Continue the melting process so the scoops lose their spherical shape and the liquid pool expands significantly across the surface.
Complete the melting until the scoops collapse into a thick, blended puddle of chocolate and vanilla liquid, with only small solid remnants remaining.

explain: Heat causes the outer layers of the ice cream to undergo a phase change from solid to liquid, creating a small pool at the contact point with the surface due to gravity.
As the internal temperature rises, the solid crystalline structure weakens, leading to further liquefaction and the spreading of the melt across the flat plane.
The remaining solid cores lose all structural support and merge into the surrounding liquid pool, increasing the total volume and surface area of the puddle.
invariants: Color palette of chocolate, vanilla, and caramel

instruction.global: Animate the rising steam from the boiling pot, showing natural turbulent motion and dispersion.
steps: Shift the steam plumes slightly to the right and extend the vapor trails upwards.
Increase the density of the steam on the left side and create more intricate swirling patterns in the center.
Thin out the steam at the very top and shift the main vapor column back toward the center of the pot.

explain: Hot vapor rises due to buoyancy and is pushed by subtle lateral air currents.
Turbulence in the air above the pot causes the steam to form chaotic, swirling vortices.
Continued evaporation maintains the steam column while ambient air flow redistributes the vapor particles.
invariants: Position and appearance of the black pot

instruction.global: Dissolve the sugar crystals into the coffee foam until they are mostly submerged and wet.
steps: Begin the dissolution process by making the sugar crystals absorb liquid and lose their bright white opacity.
Further dissolve the sugar, causing the crystals to lose their sharp edges and sink deeper into the foam.
Complete the dissolution until the sugar crystals are almost entirely integrated into the foam, appearing as a wet, brownish slush.

explain: Capillary action draws coffee liquid into the gaps between sugar crystals, initiating dissolution and changing their refractive index.
As the sugar dissolves, the solid structure of the pile collapses and the crystals sink due to gravity and loss of solid volume.
The sugar crystals reach a state of high saturation and mostly liquefy, blending with the coffee foam's texture and color.

instruction.global: Tin the soldering iron tip by melting a small amount of solder wire onto it and then removing the wire.
steps: Bring a thin solder wire from the left into contact with the heated tip of the soldering iron.
Melt the end of the solder wire against the hot tip, generating a plume of white smoke from the vaporizing flux.
Withdraw the solder wire and clear the smoke, leaving a fresh, shiny bead of solder on the iron's tip.

explain: The solder wire is positioned against the heated metal tip to initiate heat transfer.
The high temperature of the iron melts the solder alloy and vaporizes the internal flux core, creating visible smoke.
The wire is removed after depositing material, and the smoke dissipates as the flux finishes burning off, leaving a tinned surface.
invariants: Position and orientation of the soldering iron shaft.

instruction.global: Pour hot water over dry coffee grounds in a pour-over filter until the filter is filled with a blooming coffee foam.
steps: Introduce a gooseneck kettle spout and pour a stream of hot water onto the center of the dry grounds, causing them to wet and start foaming.
Continue pouring water to raise the liquid level in the filter, increasing the volume of light brown foam and bubbles.
Remove the kettle and allow the foam to settle into a smooth, thick layer at the top of the filter.

explain: Hot water hits the dry grounds, releasing trapped carbon dioxide which creates a light brown foam known as the bloom.
The continuous flow of water increases the hydrostatic pressure and volume, lifting the grounds and foam higher within the mesh filter.
With the cessation of the water stream, surface turbulence stops, allowing the bubbles to stabilize into a uniform layer of crema-like foam.
invariants: The black and silver coffee carafe base
The circular metal mesh filter frame

instruction.global: Show a water droplet forming at the tip of the icicle, elongating, and then detaching to fall downward.
steps: Accumulate a small, rounded bead of liquid water at the bottom tip of the icicle.
Elongate the water droplet as it grows heavier, pulling it downward into a teardrop shape.
Stretch the connection into a thin neck until the droplet detaches and begins to fall, leaving a small residual amount of water at the tip.

explain: Melting ice causes water to pool at the lowest point due to gravity and surface tension.
As the mass of the water increases, the gravitational force begins to overcome surface tension, causing the drop to hang and stretch.
The weight of the droplet exceeds the surface tension's holding capacity, resulting in Rayleigh-Taylor instability where the liquid neck breaks and the drop falls.
invariants: Consistent rim lighting on the ice
The structure and texture of the upper icicle body

instruction.global: Show a water droplet forming at the tip of the icicle, detaching, and falling out of frame.
steps: Accumulate a spherical water droplet at the bottom tip of the icicle.
Detach the droplet from the icicle tip and move it vertically downward.
Remove the falling droplet from the frame and restore the icicle tip to its resting state.

explain: Meltwater flows down the icicle and accumulates at the tip due to surface tension, forming a droplet.
The weight of the droplet overcomes the surface tension holding it to the ice, causing it to break away and begin free fall.
Gravity accelerates the detached droplet downward until it exits the camera's field of view.
invariants: Main icicle body structure and internal air bubbles

instruction.global: Show the continuous process of melted chocolate dripping and falling from the edge of a chocolate bar.
steps: Elongate the chocolate droplet on the right side as it stretches downward under its own weight.
Detach the falling droplet completely and smooth the remaining melted chocolate edge.
Form a new, thin droplet of chocolate descending from the center-right of the melted mass.

explain: Gravity pulls the viscous melted chocolate downward, causing the droplet to stretch and thin at the neck.
The cohesive forces of the liquid are overcome by gravity, leading to the droplet detaching and falling away.
Continued fluid flow causes chocolate to accumulate at a new low point, initiating a subsequent gravitational drip.
invariants: The structural shape and position of the solid chocolate bar
Consistent glossy lighting and reflections on the chocolate surface

instruction.global: Animate the melting chocolate bar so that the liquid chocolate drips further down under gravity.
steps: Elongate the drip on the right side and let the main bottom drip detach a droplet, making it appear shorter.
Continue the downward flow of the chocolate, making the bottom drip grow longer again.
Stretch the bottom drip into a thin, long stream and further elongate the drip on the right.

explain: Gravity pulls the viscous chocolate down, causing the right drip to stretch while the bottom drip reaches a breaking point and sheds a drop.
The liquid chocolate continues to accumulate at the lowest points, causing the bottom drip to reform and extend downwards.
Persistent gravitational pull causes the liquid to thin out into a long filament as it falls.
invariants: Solid chocolate bar structure on the left

instruction.global: Fill the upper chamber of the moka pot with brewed coffee as steam rises from the central column.
steps: Increase the volume of coffee at the base of the central column and add more swirling steam.
Raise the coffee level halfway up the reservoir while intensifying the steam density.
Fill the upper chamber almost to the top with dark coffee, maintaining the steam and surface bubbles.

explain: Steam pressure in the bottom chamber forces hot water through the coffee grounds and up the central tube.
The brewed coffee continues to flow out of the column, increasing the liquid volume in the upper reservoir.
As the brewing process nears completion, the reservoir fills with dark coffee while steam continues to escape.
invariants: Moka pot structure and metallic material
Lighting and reflections on the metal surface

instruction.global: Show the candle burning down from its full length to a short stub near the base.
steps: Reduce the height of the candle slightly and add a charred, curling wick extending from the side of the flame.
Further shorten the candle body and extend the charred wick into a circular loop.
Burn the candle down until only a small portion of the wax remains above the white base, maintaining the flame and the looped wick.

explain: As the wax is consumed by combustion, the candle shortens and the exposed wick chars and begins to bend.
Continued burning further reduces the candle's height while the charred wick curls due to heat and structural changes.
The candle reaches its final stages of combustion, leaving only a stub as the wax is nearly depleted.
invariants: White candle holder at the bottom
Spiral texture on the candle wax

instruction.global: Melt the pile of crushed ice completely until only a small residue remains on the surface.
steps: Partially melt the crushed ice, reducing the height of the peaks and forming a thin layer of water at the base.
Continue the melting process so that the larger ice chunks shrink significantly and the overall volume of solid ice decreases.
Melt the remaining small ice fragments until the surface is nearly clear, leaving only a faint trace of slush or water.

explain: Thermal energy causes the outer layers of the ice crystals to undergo a phase change from solid to liquid, resulting in structural collapse and runoff.
Continued heat absorption further reduces the surface area of the remaining ice solids, causing them to shrink and lose their sharp edges.
The final phase change completes as the last bits of solid ice reach their melting point and turn into liquid water.

instruction.global: Pour water onto dry ice in a glass bowl to create a dense, billowing fog that overflows and covers the surface.
steps: Increase the water flow and generate thick white vapor clouds that rise from the bowl and spill over the edges.
Expand the volume of the fog so it forms a large, turbulent mound and begins to spread across the dark surface.
Further increase the density and height of the fog until it obscures the bowl and pitcher, creating a thick layer of low-lying mist.

explain: Sublimation of dry ice accelerates as water is added, causing a rapid release of CO2 gas which traps moisture to form thick white fog.
The cold CO2 gas is denser than the surrounding air, causing the fog to sink and flow over the rim of the container onto the table surface.
Continuous sublimation creates a high-volume cloud that pushes outward and upward, eventually masking the reaction source and filling the lower frame.
invariants: Presence of the glass bowl and pitcher

instruction.global: Simulate the continuous sublimation and turbulent dispersal of dry ice fog rising from a bowl.
steps: Shift the central plume of fog to the right while maintaining the dense overflow at the base of the bowl.
Disperse the vertical plumes into more chaotic, swirling wisps with reduced height.
Settle the fog closer to the surface, making the rising plumes less defined and more fragmented.

explain: Convection and ambient air currents cause the dense carbon dioxide fog to swirl and change its flow pattern.
Turbulent mixing with the surrounding air breaks the cohesive rising column into smaller, more dispersed vapor clouds.
As the gas loses momentum and mixes further, the fog becomes less concentrated in the center and spreads horizontally.

instruction.global: Melt the chocolate coating off the ice cream bar until the inner cream core is mostly exposed.
steps: Increase the melting of the chocolate coating, causing it to slide downwards and reveal more of the cream-colored interior.
Continue the melting process, forming a distinct, long drip of chocolate from the bottom-left tip.
Shed the majority of the outer chocolate layer, leaving only thin streaks of chocolate on the surface of the melting ice cream core.

explain: Heat causes the chocolate shell to soften and lose adhesion to the ice cream, while gravity pulls the liquid chocolate downwards.
Surface tension and gravity cause the accumulated melted chocolate at the bottom to form a droplet and begin to fall away from the bar.
The outer chocolate layer has largely sloughed off due to melting, and the inner ice cream core begins to liquefy and drip as well.

instruction.global: Render the melting process of a popsicle, showing the formation, elongation, and detachment of liquid drips.
steps: Elongate the existing droplets at the bottom of the popsicle into long, thin drips.
Remove the elongated drips as if they have fallen, and form new, small rounded droplets along the bottom edge.
Extend the droplet at the front tip into a single sharp drip while smoothing the other small beads of liquid.

explain: Gravity pulls the melting cream downwards, causing the droplets to stretch into long, thin columns due to the liquid's viscosity.
The weight of the drips overcomes surface tension, causing them to detach and fall, leaving behind small residual beads of liquid on the surface.
Continued melting causes liquid to accumulate at the lowest point of the popsicle's curvature, forming a new primary drip.
invariants: Position and texture of the wooden popsicle stick
Overall color and speckled pattern of the popsicle coating

instruction.global: Melt the stick of butter in the black pan until it is mostly liquid and covered in white foam.
steps: Begin melting the butter stick, surrounding it with a layer of white, bubbly foam on the pan surface.
Shrink the solid butter stick slightly and expand the area of the white foam bubbles across the pan.
Significantly reduce the size of the remaining solid butter stick and fill the pan with a dense layer of small white bubbles.

explain: Heat from the pan causes the solid butter to undergo a phase change into liquid, while water content evaporates to create foam.
Continued thermal energy transfer further liquefies the solid mass, increasing the volume of liquid and the density of the surface bubbles.
The solid core continues to dissolve into the liquid phase as the emulsion breaks down, resulting in a thick layer of foam and a smaller solid remnant.
invariants: Pale yellow color of the butter

instruction.global: Show the ice cube melting on a dark reflective surface, with the ice mass decreasing and a water puddle spreading outward.
steps: Slightly reduce the size of the ice cube and begin spreading a thin layer of meltwater to the left on the reflective surface.
Further shrink the ice cube, rounding its sharp edges, and expand the water puddle significantly towards the left.
Continue the melting process by making the ice cube smaller and extending the puddle of water further along the surface.

explain: Thermal energy from the environment causes the outer layer of the ice to undergo a phase transition from solid to liquid.
As melting continues, the volume of the solid ice decreases while the volume of liquid water increases, flowing outward due to gravity and surface tension.
The ice cube continues to lose mass as it absorbs heat, resulting in a larger footprint of meltwater on the non-porous surface.
